Bee Genus Field Guide

Identification · Characteristics · Verified Sources · Tree-nesting species only

© CBG Photography Group 2016  ·  CC BY 4.0  ·  Pinned specimen, lateral view

Genus

Axestotrigona

African Stingless Bees

Family: Apidae Tribe: Meliponini ~8 species

A genus of small African stingless bees lacking yellow integumental markings. Highly eusocial and producing honey. Found in tropical African forest habitats; often treated as a subgenus of Meliponula.

Body length — scale 0–30 mm

adult
5mm
7mm
honeybee
11mm
15mm
bumblebee
16mm
23mm
Adult
Honeybee / Bumblebee reference
🔲

Key marking

Small, uniformly dark bees lacking yellow integumental markings. Dull, matte integument. Hind tibia keirotrichiate zone extends to the retromarginal edge — a key diagnostic.

🪶

Body & hair

Small and compact. Distinguished from related stingless bee genera by specific hind tibia features requiring close examination.

🌍

Distribution

Sub-Saharan tropical Africa — West, Central, and East Africa including Tanzania, Cameroon, and Ghana.

🏠

Nesting

Nests in tree hollows or abandoned structures. Entrance is a bee-sized hole on a short rounded cerumen platform. Honey and pollen stored in separate spherical pots. Colonies can be large.

🐝

Sociality

Highly eusocial with a queen, workers, and drones. Workers produce vibrational alarm signals in defense. Honey is of good quality.

Key field identification points

  • Very small (5–7 mm), dark, uniformly coloured — no yellow integumental markings
  • Stingless — workers can bite but cannot sting
  • Nest entrance is a short rounded cerumen platform on a tree hollow
  • Distinguished from Liotrigona by dull rather than shiny integument

Verification sources

  • PMC — Stingless bee classification and biologyhttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10401200/
  • Springer — Nest architecture of Meliponula (Axestotrigona) ferrugineahttps://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12038-018-9840-8
  • NCBI — Guarding vibrations in Axestotrigona ferrugineahttps://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8146115/

Stingless Bee · Meliponini

Hypotrigona ruspolii

Ruspoli's Minute Stingless Bee

Family: Apidae Tribe: Meliponini Genus: Hypotrigona

A minute stingless bee of East African indigenous forests. H. ruspolii nests in tree cavities and is associated with mature indigenous forest habitats. Among the smallest bees in Africa; colonies are small and produce modest honey. Distinguished from related Hypotrigona species by its strong association with forest tree cavities rather than more exposed or degraded substrates.

Body length — scale 0–30 mm

adult
2mm
4mm
honeybee
11mm
15mm
bumblebee
16mm
23mm
Adult
Honeybee / Bumblebee reference
🔲

Key marking

Minute, dull and matte dark bee. Broadly rounded distal superior angle of the hind tibia — key Hypotrigona diagnostic. No functional sting.

🌳

Habitat

Indigenous forest. Associated with mature, closed-canopy forest in East Africa. Not typically found in degraded or open woodland.

🌍

Distribution

East Africa. Recorded from Ethiopia and Kenya. Range follows indigenous forest patches through the region.

🏠

Nesting

Tree cavity nester. Prefers hollows in living indigenous trees. Characteristic trumpet-shaped cerumen entrance tube projects from nest opening. Honey and pollen stored in spherical pots.

🐝

Sociality

Eusocial; colonies typically small. Modest honey production. Honey has traditional medicinal uses in local communities across East Africa.

🔬

ID challenge

Difficult to separate from other Hypotrigona species in the field. Distinguish from Liotrigona by duller (less shiny) integument. Specialist examination required for species-level confirmation.

Key field identification points

  • Minute size (2–4 mm) — among Africa's smallest bees
  • Dull matte dark integument — less shiny than Liotrigona
  • Tree cavity nest in indigenous forest — habitat is a primary field cue
  • Trumpet-shaped cerumen entrance tube at nest opening
  • Stingless — cannot sting but may bite
  • Photo pending — consult specialist sources for species-level confirmation

Verification sources

  • PMC — Stingless bee classification and biologyhttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10401200/
  • Lucid Central — Hypotrigona bees fact sheethttps://keys.lucidcentral.org/keys/v3/eafrinet/bee_genera/key/african_bee_genera/Media/Html_eafrica/Hypotrigona_bees.htm

Genus

Apis mellifera scutellata

African / Savannah Honey Bee

Family: Apidae Tribe: Apini 1 subspecies

The African or savannah honey bee — one of two honey bee subspecies native to South Africa. Found across the savannah and bushveld north of the Cape Fold mountains. Known internationally as the source of the 'Africanised' or 'killer' bee after introduction to Brazil in 1957. Highly defensive, prolific, and ecologically important.

Body length — scale 0–30 mm

adult
10mm
13mm
honeybee
11mm
15mm
bumblebee
16mm
23mm
Adult
Honeybee / Bumblebee reference
🟡

Key marking

Light-coloured phenotype. Workers have a yellow-orange banded abdomen with black stripes — lighter overall than A. m. capensis. Thorax covered in short pale hair. Corbicula (pollen basket) on hind legs.

🪶

Body & hair

Compact typical honey bee form. Workers 10–13 mm. Slightly smaller on average than capensis but the difference is too small for reliable field use. Short sparse hair.

🌍

Distribution

Native to central and southern Africa; dominant across most of South Africa north of the Cape Fold mountains. A stable hybrid zone exists where it meets capensis to the south.

🏠

Nesting

Nests in natural cavities — tree hollows, rock crevices, roof spaces, occasionally underground. Strongly prone to swarming and absconding. May usurp existing colonies. Nest entrance heavily guarded.

⚠️

Behaviour

Highly defensive — responds to disturbance with large numbers of stinging workers pursuing intruders much further than European bees. Defensive traits are ecological adaptations, not random aggression.

Key field identification points

  • Lighter yellow-orange banded abdomen — less black than capensis on average
  • Found north of the Cape Fold mountains in savannah and bushveld habitat
  • Highly defensive around the nest; large numbers of bees respond to disturbance
  • Prone to swarming and absconding — colonies may disappear suddenly
  • Important caveat: the two South African subspecies are considered visually near-indistinguishable by most scientific sources — reliable separation requires tergite colour scoring and morphometrics
  • In the hybrid zone (Eastern Cape), confident field ID to subspecies level is not possible

Verification sources

  • Wikipedia — Apis mellifera scutellatahttps://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apis_mellifera_scutellata
  • SANBI — African honeybeehttps://www.sanbi.org/animal-of-the-week/african-honeybee/
  • PMC — Population genomics of South African honey beeshttps://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6094452/
  • Springer — Morphometric comparison capensis vs scutellatahttps://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13592-019-00651-6

Genus

Apis mellifera capensis

Cape Honey Bee

Family: Apidae Tribe: Apini 1 subspecies

The Cape honey bee — endemic to the Western and Eastern Cape provinces of South Africa (the fynbos region). One of the most biologically remarkable bee subspecies in the world: workers can lay diploid female eggs without mating (thelytoky), enabling them to act as social parasites of A. m. scutellata colonies — the so-called 'capensis calamity'.

Body length — scale 0–30 mm

adult
11mm
14mm
honeybee
11mm
15mm
bumblebee
16mm
23mm
Adult
Honeybee / Bumblebee reference
🔲

Key marking

Darker phenotype than A. m. scutellata. Workers have an almost entirely black abdomen with reduced yellow banding. Tergite colour is the most reliable visual distinction between the two South African subspecies.

🪶

Body & hair

Compact typical honey bee form. Workers 11–14 mm — marginally larger on average than scutellata workers but the difference is too small for reliable field use. Short sparse hair. Corbicula on hind legs.

🌍

Distribution

Endemic to the Western Cape and Eastern Cape provinces. Confined to the coastal side of the Cape Fold mountain range (the fynbos biome). A stable hybrid zone separates it from A. m. scutellata to the north.

🏠

Nesting

Nests in natural cavities — tree hollows, rock crevices, roof spaces. Less prone to swarming and absconding than scutellata. Generally more manageable for beekeeping within its native fynbos range. Important pollinator of fynbos and Cape agricultural crops.

🧬

Unique biology

Workers reproduce thelytokously — laying diploid female eggs without mating. Workers can develop into pseudoqueens mimicking queen pheromones. When capensis workers infiltrate scutellata colonies (often via migratory beekeeping) they parasitise and ultimately kill the host colony.

Key field identification points

  • Darker abdomen — almost entirely black with reduced yellow banding — the most useful visual cue vs scutellata
  • Found south of the Cape Fold mountains in fynbos, Western Cape, and Eastern Cape
  • Less defensive than scutellata — more manageable behaviour around the nest
  • Tergite (abdominal segment) colour is the most reliable field distinction between the two subspecies
  • Important caveat: the two South African subspecies are considered visually near-indistinguishable by most scientific sources — definitive separation requires morphometrics, not casual observation
  • In the hybrid zone (Eastern Cape), confident field ID to subspecies level is not possible

Verification sources

  • Wikipedia — Cape honey beehttps://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cape_honey_bee
  • BeeAware — Cape honeybeehttps://beeaware.org.au/archive-pest/cape-honey-bee/
  • PMC — Genetics of colour traits in SA honey beeshttps://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8670704/
  • SABIO — Honeybees of South Africahttps://sabio.org.za/honeybees-of-south-africa/
  • Springer — Morphometric comparison capensis vs scutellatahttps://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13592-019-00651-6

Stingless Bee · Meliponini

Dactylurina schmidti

Schmidt's Stingless Bee

Family: Apidae Tribe: Meliponini Genus: Dactylurina

The most visually distinctive African stingless bee — identifiable by its massive exposed nest on tree trunks and its unique laterally compressed abdomen. Produces honey with the strongest antimicrobial properties of any East African stingless bee species. Found across East Africa including Tanzania, Kenya, and likely Zambia.

Worker size

D. schmidti
5mm
7mm
Honeybee
11mm
15mm
📐

Body size

5–7 mm. Slender build.

🎨

Colouration

Entirely black. Face completely and densely clothed with strongly plumose white vestiture — key field mark.

🔬

Diagnostic character

Laterally compressed metasoma — abdomen longer than broad, more than 3× scutum length. Long hind legs (tibia more than 2× scutum length). Partly convex corbicula.

🏠

Nest type

Exposed vertical comb on tree trunk or branches — surrounded by batumen (resin, mud, wax). Large dark structure covering significant area of bark. Highly distinctive — cannot be confused with Apis nest.

🍯

Honey

Stored in cerumen pots inside nest. Tangy, acidic — higher moisture than Apis honey. Strongest antimicrobial activity of any East African stingless bee species. Significant medicinal value.

🌍

Distribution

East Africa — confirmed Kenya, Tanzania. Likely present in Zambia based on Miombo habitat range. Separated from D. staudingeri (West/Central Africa) by facial vestiture and distribution.

🍃

Foraging

Collects both nectar and pollen. Foraging duration ~2× longer per flower than Apis mellifera. Starts foraging readily in new environments.

🧬

Voucher collection

Ethanol vials (2-dram). Workers accessible at nest entrance. Nest material (batumen) sample also useful. DNA barcoding via COI gene recommended for confirmation.

Field Identification Key

  • Massive dark exposed nest on tree trunk or branches — unmistakable, no other species builds like this
  • Worker: entirely black, 5–7mm, slender with laterally compressed abdomen
  • Dense white plumose facial hair — visible with hand lens
  • Long hind legs relative to body size
  • Nest surface dark brown/black with amber resin patches — local description "black and yellow" likely refers to resin colouration
  • Confirmed by nest architecture alone — no other African stingless bee builds an exposed external nest of this scale

Sources

  • TaxonomyEardley CD (2004) — Taxonomic revision of the African stingless bees. African Plant Protection 10(2): 63–96
  • Honey antimicrobial propertiesAntimicrobial Properties of Propolis and Honey from the Kenyan Stingless bee Dactylurina schmidti
  • East Africa species accountsStingless honey-beekeeping around Udzungwa Mountains NP, Tanzania (2010)
  • Kenya morphometrics + DNAIdentification of stingless bees in Kenya using morphometrics and DNA barcoding (2017)

© Subramanian Sevgan  ·  CC BY-NC 4.0  ·  iNaturalist — reddish-brown colour form, foraging worker

Stingless Bee · Meliponini

Meliponula ferruginea

Rusty Stingless Bee

Family: Apidae Tribe: Meliponini Subgenus: Axestotrigona

The most widespread African stingless bee, found across all geographic and ecological zones. Exists in two distinct colour forms — reddish-brown and black — which can cause confusion with other species. Tree cavity nester. Widely used in meliponiculture across East Africa.

🎨

Colouration

Two colour forms: reddish-brown (ferruginous — distinctive and easily identified) and black form (harder to separate from other species). The reddish-brown form is the most visually distinctive Meliponula in the field.

🏠

Nest type

Tree cavity nester. Entrance tube of cerumen projecting from cavity entrance. Also recorded in termite nests. Does not build exposed nests.

🔬

ID challenge

Black form clusters morphometrically with Dactylurina schmidti in PCA — despite being a different genus. Black form requires microscopy or DNA for confident ID. Reddish-brown form is field-identifiable by colour alone.

🌍

Distribution

Most widespread African stingless bee — found across all 7 geographic zones studied in Tanzania. Present in forest, savanna, and farmland. Very likely present at Kasanka sites.

🍯

Honey

Honey and pollen stored in cerumen pots — yellow/amber wax pots visible when hive opened. "Black and yellow hive" description likely refers to dark brood comb + yellow cerumen pots.

🧬

Voucher collection

Ethanol vials (2-dram). Collect both colour forms separately if encountered. DNA barcoding strongly recommended for black form.

Field Identification Key

  • Reddish-brown form — distinctive amber/orange colouration, no other Meliponula matches this
  • Black form — tree cavity nest with cerumen entrance tube; requires microscopy or DNA
  • Hind tibia strongly concave (outer surface) — separates Meliponula genus from Plebeina and Dactylurina
  • Forewing with vein Rs visible — separates from Hypotrigona
  • "Black and yellow" interior when hive opened — dark brood comb + yellow cerumen pots

Sources

  • TaxonomyEardley CD (2004) — Taxonomic revision of the African stingless bees
  • Kenya morphometrics + DNAIdentification of stingless bees in Kenya using morphometrics and DNA barcoding (2017)
  • Tanzania honey physicochemistryDiscrimination of honeys produced by Tanzanian stingless bees (2023)

© Nganso T. Beatrice  ·  CC BY-NC 4.0  ·  iNaturalist — workers at nest entrance, yellow-green facial markings visible

Stingless Bee · Meliponini

Meliponula lendliana

Lendl's Stingless Bee · Local name (Tanzania): Limbofu

Family: Apidae Tribe: Meliponini Genus: Meliponula

A tree cavity nesting stingless bee with high honey productivity — the second most productive of the six East African stingless bee species. Morphologically very similar to Plebeina hildebrandti; the two cannot be reliably separated by morphology alone and DNA barcoding is required for definitive ID. A useful field distinction is the presence of yellow-green facial markings in M. lendliana, which are absent or much reduced in P. hildebrandti. Known to local Tanzanian communities as Limbofu.

🏠

Nest type

Tree cavity nester. Cerumen entrance tube. Does not build exposed nests.

🍯

Honey productivity

Average 2.7L per colony — second highest of six East African stingless bee species after M. ogouensis (3.2L).

🔬

ID challenge

Very similar to Plebeina hildebrandti — clusters with it in PCA. Best field distinction: yellow-green facial markings present in M. lendliana, absent or much reduced in P. hildebrandti. DNA barcoding (COI gene) still required for definitive confirmation.

🌍

Distribution

East and Central Africa. Recorded in Tanzania lowlands. Likely present across Miombo woodland range including Zambia.

👥

Local knowledge

Known as Limbofu in Tanzania (Udzungwa area). Local communities distinguish this species — their identification is likely more reliable than morphological assessment in the field.

🧬

Voucher collection

Ethanol vials (2-dram). DNA barcoding essential for confirmation. Collect separately from P. hildebrandti if community members can distinguish them.

Field Identification Key

  • Tree cavity nest with cerumen entrance tube
  • Hind tibia strongly concave — Meliponula genus confirmed
  • Yellow-green facial markings — present in M. lendliana, absent or much reduced in Plebeina hildebrandti; most reliable visual field distinction between the two species
  • DNA barcoding (COI gene) required for definitive species-level confirmation
  • Local name (Limbofu) and community knowledge remains a valuable field distinction — record separately from morphological assessment

Sources

  • TaxonomyEardley CD (2004) — Taxonomic revision of the African stingless bees
  • Tanzania species accounts + honey productivityStingless honey-beekeeping around Udzungwa Mountains NP, Tanzania (2010)
  • Morphometrics + DNAIdentification of stingless bees in Kenya using morphometrics and DNA barcoding (2017)

Stingless Bee · Meliponini

Meliponula ogouensis

Ogooué Stingless Bee · Local name (Tanzania): Mlenga

Family: Apidae Tribe: Meliponini Genus: Meliponula

The highest honey-producing stingless bee species in East Africa. Tree cavity nester with a distinctive internal nest architecture — cylindrical brood cells layered irregularly. Known to local Tanzanian communities as Mlenga. A strong candidate for meliponiculture development given honey yield.

🏠

Nest type

Tree cavity nester. Distinctive internal architecture: brood cells cylindrical and layered irregularly — useful diagnostic when nest is opened or harvested.

🍯

Honey productivity

Average 3.2L per colony — highest of all six East African stingless bee species studied. Significant commercial potential.

🔬

Key diagnostic

Cylindrical, irregularly layered brood cells visible when nest is opened during harvest. This internal nest architecture is distinctive among the cavity-nesting species.

🌍

Distribution

Named for the Ogooué River in Gabon — Central and East Africa. Recorded in Tanzania lowland sites. Likely present in Zambia Miombo woodland.

👥

Local knowledge

Known as Mlenga in Tanzania. Community identification is valuable — beekeepers who harvest regularly can distinguish by nest interior and bee behaviour.

🧬

Voucher collection

Ethanol vials (2-dram). Note internal nest architecture in field records. DNA barcoding recommended for confirmation alongside morphometrics.

Field Identification Key

  • Tree cavity nest with cerumen entrance tube
  • Internal nest: cylindrical brood cells in irregular layers — visible when hive opened during harvest
  • Hind tibia strongly concave — Meliponula genus confirmed
  • Highest honey productivity of cavity nesters — beekeepers may know this colony type as particularly productive
  • Local name Mlenga (Tanzania) — ask for equivalent local name at Kasanka sites

Sources

  • Tanzania species accounts + honey productivityStingless honey-beekeeping around Udzungwa Mountains NP, Tanzania (2010)
  • TaxonomyEardley CD (2004) — Taxonomic revision of the African stingless bees

Appendix

Morphometric identification

Apis mellifera capensis vs A. m. scutellata

The two South African honey bee subspecies cannot be separated by casual visual inspection. Reliable differentiation requires formal morphometric analysis of worker bees using a dissecting microscope, calibrated imaging software, and a minimum sample of 10 workers per colony. The ten characters below, derived from Hepburn et al. (1998) as applied to South African subspecies, are the standard set used in published research.

What you need

🔬

Equipment

Dissecting microscope (10–40×); calibrated digital camera or USB microscope; imaging software (ImageJ is free and widely used); fine forceps; 70% ethanol for specimen preservation.

🐝

Specimens

Minimum 10 worker bees per colony, ideally 20. Collect foragers directly from the hive entrance — avoid nurse bees (smaller, less melanised). Store in 70% ethanol or freeze immediately.

📋

Approach

Measure all 10 characters below from each worker. Average across the sample of workers to get a colony mean. Compare colony means against published reference values, or enter data into DrawWing or Beemorpho software for automated classification.

The ten diagnostic characters

01

Tergite A3 pigmentation — most discriminating character

What it is: The degree of black pigmentation on the 3rd abdominal tergite (dorsal abdominal plate). Scored on the Ruttner 0–9 scale: 0 = fully black, 9 = no black (fully yellow/pale).

How to measure: Remove the abdomen and place tergite A3 flat under the microscope. Compare against the Ruttner reference chart and assign a score.

What to expect: A. m. scutellata scores higher (more yellow; typically 4–7). A. m. capensis scores lower (more black; typically 0–3). This single character provides the strongest separation of the two subspecies.

02

Scutellum pigmentation

What it is: Pigmentation of the scutellum — the triangular plate on the dorsal thorax between the wing bases. Scored on a 0–5 scale (0 = fully black, 5 = fully pale/yellow).

How to measure: View the thorax dorsally under the microscope and assign a score from the reference chart.

What to expect: Scutellata tends to score higher (more pale). Capensis tends to score lower (darker). Used in combination with tergite pigmentation for improved accuracy.

03

Scutellar plate pigmentation

What it is: Pigmentation of the scutellar plate (the smaller plate immediately behind the scutellum). Scored on the same 0–9 Ruttner scale.

How to measure: View the thorax dorsally; score the scutellar plate separately from the scutellum itself.

What to expect: Same directional trend as scutellum — scutellata paler, capensis darker. Together, characters 1–3 form the core pigmentation battery.

04

Forewing length

What it is: The total length of the right forewing, measured from the base of the costal vein to the wingtip. Expressed in mm.

How to measure: Remove the right forewing and place flat on a slide. Photograph under calibrated microscope and measure in ImageJ from the base of costa to the wing apex.

What to expect: Capensis workers tend to have slightly longer forewings on average than scutellata, though there is considerable overlap. Typical range for African subspecies: 8.0–9.5 mm.

05

Forewing angle N23

What it is: The angle at vein junction N23 in the forewing (a specific intersection point in the wing venation network, numbered according to Ruttner's system). Measured in degrees.

How to measure: Photograph the right forewing flat under the microscope. In ImageJ or DrawWing software, mark the N23 junction and the two veins meeting at it, then measure the angle. DrawWing software can automate this from a photograph.

What to expect: Differs between subspecies. Used as part of the multivariate wing venation profile rather than as a standalone character.

06

Forewing angle O26

What it is: The angle at vein junction O26 in the forewing. The second of the two key venation angles for distinguishing South African subspecies.

How to measure: As for N23 — photograph the forewing and measure in imaging software.

What to expect: Combined with N23, these two angles provide the main wing venation discrimination. DrawWing and Beemorpho software handle both angles simultaneously from a single forewing photograph.

07

Wax plate (sternite A4) — transverse width

What it is: The width of the wax mirror (wax-secreting plate) on the underside of abdominal sternite 4, measured across its widest point. Expressed in mm.

How to measure: Remove the abdomen, expose the ventral surface, and photograph sternite A4 flat. Measure the widest transverse dimension of the wax plate in ImageJ.

What to expect: Wax plate dimensions differ between subspecies and contribute to overall body size discrimination.

08

Wax plate (sternite A4) — transverse length

What it is: The length of the same wax mirror on sternite A4, measured along its long axis. Expressed in mm.

How to measure: Same preparation as character 7 — measure the longitudinal dimension of the wax plate.

What to expect: Used in combination with wax plate width. Both wax plate measurements reflect overall body size and contribute to multivariate separation of the subspecies.

09

Cover hair length on tergite A6

What it is: The length of the longest cover hairs (overhairs) on the 6th abdominal tergite. Expressed in mm.

How to measure: Under the microscope, identify the longest cover hairs on tergite A6 (the rear-most visible dorsal abdominal plate). Measure several and record the mean maximum length.

What to expect: Hair length varies between subspecies and is part of the pilosity (hairiness) profile that contributes to overall subspecies discrimination.

10

Ovariole number — dissection required

What it is: The number of ovarioles (egg-producing tubes) per ovary in a worker bee. Counted under the microscope after dissection.

How to measure: Carefully dissect the abdomen in saline solution under a microscope. Remove the ovaries and tease them apart gently. Count the number of ovarioles on one or both ovaries.

What to expect: This is the most invasive measurement and requires dissection skill. A. m. capensis workers have significantly more ovarioles (mean ~7) than scutellata workers (mean ~2–3) — reflecting their unique reproductive capacity. This character alone provides a strong signal but requires care to avoid damaging the tissue.

Practical notes

Key points for reliable analysis

  • No single character reliably separates the two subspecies on its own — all 10 characters are used together in multivariate analysis
  • The tergite A3 pigmentation score (character 01) is the single most discriminating character — always include it
  • Always measure a minimum of 10 workers per colony; 20 is recommended for reliable colony means
  • Measurements must come from foragers (field bees), not nurse bees, to avoid within-colony size variation
  • In the hybrid zone (Eastern Cape) colonies may be mixed — morphometric results in this area should be interpreted cautiously
  • The free software DrawWing (drawwing.org) and Beemorpho can automate wing angle measurement from photographs and are recommended for wing venation analysis
  • Genetic analysis (DNA) remains the most definitive method — morphometrics supports but does not replace it

Sources for this appendix

  • Hepburn et al. (1998) — 8 diagnostic characters for capensis vs scutellata (via Springer)https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13592-019-00651-6
  • BMC Genomics — 10 morphometric characters for SA subspecieshttps://bmcgenomics.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12864-018-4998-x
  • Ruttner (1988) pigmentation scale reference — DrawWinghttps://honeybee.drawwing.org/book/cubital-index
  • MDPI — Quantifying abdominal coloration in worker honey beeshttps://www.mdpi.com/2075-4450/15/4/213
  • DrawWing software (free, forewing morphometrics)https://drawwing.org
  • PMC — XGBoost-based morphometric classification of Apis melliferahttps://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12842149/
